file_purpose: Proves that the Bellare-Rogaway 1993 (BR93) public-key encryption scheme is IND-CPA secure under the assumption that the underlying trapdoor permutation is one-way. The proof is first carried out in an abstract theory with minimal algebraic structure, then instantiated to fixed-length bitstrings.

main_theorem.name: Reduction (concrete, line 584)
main_theorem.informal_statement: For any IND-CPA adversary A against the concrete BR scheme (bitstring instantiation), the CPA advantage Pr[CPA(LRO, BR, A) : res] - 1/2 is bounded by the one-way inversion advantage Pr[Exp_OW(I(A, LRO)) : res].

proof_strategy_overview: The proof uses a three-hop game sequence G0 -> G1 -> G2 -> OW. Step 1 (G0 to G1) replaces the random oracle call H.o(r) during challenge encryption with an independent uniform sample h, introducing a 'bad event' that the adversary queried r to the oracle; the fundamental lemma bounds the gap by Pr[bad]. Step 2 (G1 to G2) exploits the fact that h +^ m is uniformly distributed when h is uniform, removing the XOR masking from the ciphertext; this is a tight equivalence via a bijection argument. In G2 the ciphertext is independent of the challenge bit, so Pr[G2:res] = 1/2 exactly. Step 3 reduces the bad event probability to the one-way inversion advantage: if the adversary ever queries the RO at the encryption randomness r, the constructed inverter I(A) can extract r from the query log and invert f(pk, r). The abstract result is then instantiated to concrete bitstrings by cloning the abstract theory.

game_chain.description: BR93_CPA(A): the real IND-CPA game where the adversary chooses two messages, receives an encryption of one under a random bit b using the BR93 scheme with a lazy random oracle, and guesses b.

Game1: like G0 but the random oracle call during challenge encryption is replaced by fresh uniform sampling h <$ dptxt; the encryption randomness r is stored as Game1.r to define the bad event (r in Log.qs).
Game2: like G1 but the challenge ciphertext uses (f pk r, h) instead of (f pk r, h +^ (b ? m0 : m1)); since h is already uniform, the ciphertext is now completely independent of the challenge bit b.
OW(I(A)): the one-way inversion game where the constructed inverter I(A) receives (pk, f(pk,x)) and must recover x, using the CPA adversary A's oracle queries to find x in the query log.

lemma_catalog.name: addpK
lemma_catalog.narrative: Algebraic helper: proves x +^ y +^ y = x, the cancellation law for the nilpotent XOR operation. Used in correctness proof and in the G1-G2 bijection argument.

lemma_catalog.name: dptxt_ll
lemma_catalog.narrative: Distribution property: dptxt is lossless. Used throughout for termination arguments.

lemma_catalog.name: dptxt_uni
lemma_catalog.narrative: Distribution property: dptxt is uniform.

lemma_catalog.name: dptxt_fu
lemma_catalog.narrative: Distribution property: dptxt is full (has full support).

lemma_catalog.name: dptxt_funi
lemma_catalog.narrative: Distribution property: dptxt is funiform (full + uniform). Key for the bijection argument in eq_Game1_Game2.

lemma_catalog.name: drand_ll
lemma_catalog.narrative: Distribution property: drand is lossless.

lemma_catalog.name: drand_uni
lemma_catalog.narrative: Distribution property: drand is uniform.

lemma_catalog.name: dkeys_ll
lemma_catalog.narrative: Distribution property: dkeys is lossless.

lemma_catalog.name: dkeys_funi
lemma_catalog.narrative: Distribution property: dkeys is funiform.

lemma_catalog.name: fI
lemma_catalog.narrative: Injectivity of the trapdoor permutation f(pk, ·): if f(pk,x) = f(pk,y) then x = y. Derived from the fK axiom. Used in pr_Game2_OW to justify that nth_find recovers the correct preimage.

lemma_catalog.name: BR93_correct
lemma_catalog.narrative: Sanity check: BR93 decryption correctly recovers the plaintext with probability 1. Not used in the security proof but validates the scheme definition.

lemma_catalog.name: pr_Game0_Game1
lemma_catalog.narrative: First game hop via the fundamental lemma: bounds Pr[G0:res] by Pr[G1:res] + Pr[G1:bad] where bad = (Game1.r in Log.qs). The two games differ only at the point where the RO is queried at r during encryption; if the adversary has not queried r (bad does not hold), the RO output is independent and identically distributed to a fresh sample.
lemma_catalog.semantic_delta: Replaces the random oracle evaluation H.o(r) during challenge encryption with independent uniform sampling h <$ dptxt; the two games are identical conditioned on the adversary not having queried the oracle at r.

lemma_catalog.name: eq_Game1_Game2
lemma_catalog.narrative: pRHL equivalence between Game1 and Game2. Establishes that the two games produce identical distributions on (res, Log state, r). Used by pr_Game1_Game2 and pr_bad_Game1_Game2 to transfer both the advantage and the bad event probability. The bijection rnd (fun x => x +^ m) witnesses that h +^ m is uniform when h is uniform.
lemma_catalog.semantic_delta: Replaces (f pk r, h +^ (b ? m0 : m1)) with (f pk r, h) in the challenge ciphertext by coupling h in Game1 with h +^ (b ? m0 : m1) in Game2 via the XOR bijection on dptxt.

lemma_catalog.name: pr_Game1_Game2
lemma_catalog.narrative: Transfers the winning probability from Game1 to Game2: Pr[G1:res] = Pr[G2:res]. Direct corollary of eq_Game1_Game2.
lemma_catalog.semantic_delta: XOR masking with the challenge message is removed from the ciphertext; the uniform distribution of h absorbs the message, making res probabilities identical.

lemma_catalog.name: pr_bad_Game1_Game2
lemma_catalog.narrative: Transfers the bad event probability from Game1 to Game2: Pr[G1: r in Log.qs] = Pr[G2: r in Log.qs]. Direct corollary of eq_Game1_Game2 (the equivalence preserves Game1.r = Game2.r and Log.qs equality).
lemma_catalog.semantic_delta: Identical to pr_Game1_Game2 but for the bad event (r in Log.qs) rather than the winning event; the eq_Game1_Game2 equivalence couples both events simultaneously.

lemma_catalog.name: pr_Game2
lemma_catalog.narrative: Shows Pr[G2:res] = 1/2 exactly: in Game2 the challenge ciphertext (f pk r, h) is independent of the bit b, so the adversary's guess b' is independent of b and the probability of b' = b is exactly 1/2. Proved by byphoare with a swap to move the b-sampling to the end and applying dbool1E.

lemma_catalog.name: OW_OWr
lemma_catalog.narrative: Syntactic equivalence between OW(I) and OWr(I) — a reformulation of the one-way game where the challenge preimage x is stored as a module variable. The two are equivalent by sim. Used in pr_Game2_OW to work with the more convenient OWr formulation.

lemma_catalog.name: pr_Game2_OW
lemma_catalog.narrative: The reduction step: bounds Pr[G2: r in Log.qs] by Pr[OW(I(A)):res]. If the adversary ever queries the random oracle at the encryption randomness r, then r appears in Log.qs and the inverter I(A) extracts it via nth/find, successfully inverting f(pk, r). Uses fI (injectivity of f) to justify that the found query is the correct preimage.
lemma_catalog.semantic_delta: Reduces the bad event 'adversary queries RO at encryption randomness r' to successful one-way inversion: the inverter I(A) simulates Game2 for the adversary and scans the query log for a preimage of the OW challenge y = f(pk, r).

lemma_catalog.name: Reduction (abstract, line 367)
lemma_catalog.narrative: Combines all game hops: Pr[BR93_CPA(A):res] - 1/2 <= Pr[OW(I(A)):res]. Derived by chaining pr_Game0_Game1, pr_Game1_Game2, pr_bad_Game1_Game2, pr_Game2, and pr_Game2_OW via smt. This is the abstract-theory version of the security bound.

lemma_catalog.name: gt0_n
lemma_catalog.narrative: Concrete instantiation helper: ciphertext bitstring length n = l + k is positive.

lemma_catalog.name: parseK
lemma_catalog.narrative: Concrete instantiation helper: parse(r || p) = (r, p), i.e., parsing a formatted ciphertext recovers its components.

lemma_catalog.name: formatI
lemma_catalog.narrative: Concrete instantiation helper: the format operator (||) is injective.

lemma_catalog.name: Reduction (concrete, line 584)
lemma_catalog.narrative: The final concrete security theorem: for the bitstring-instantiated BR scheme and any CPA adversary A, Pr[CPA(LRO, BR, A):res] - 1/2 <= Pr[Exp_OW(I(A, LRO)):res]. Proved by instantiating the abstract BR93 theory (clone Instance) and invoking the abstract Reduction lemma via smt on the instantiated bridge lemmas.

glossary.BR93: The Bellare-Rogaway 1993 public-key encryption scheme: enc(pk, m) = (f(pk,r), H(r) +^ m) where r is random, f is a trapdoor permutation, and H is a random oracle.
glossary.BR93_CPA(A): The IND-CPA security game specialized to BR93 with a lazy random oracle; adversary A chooses two messages, receives an encryption of one, and guesses which.
glossary.LRO: Lazy Random Oracle — the standard ROM.Lazy.LRO module that lazily samples random oracle outputs on first query and caches them.
glossary.Log: Oracle wrapper that records all adversary queries to the random oracle in a list Log.qs, used to define the bad event.
glossary.OW / OW_rand: One-Way game for trapdoor permutations: given (pk, f(pk,x)), the inverter must recover x. OW_rand is the instantiation of the generic OW theory with the concrete rand type.
glossary.I(A): The constructed OW inverter built from CPA adversary A: simulates the CPA game for A, samples a random hash instead of querying the RO, then scans A's query log for the preimage of the OW challenge.
glossary.Game1: Intermediate game where the RO call H.o(r) during encryption is replaced by uniform sampling h <$ dptxt; stores r as Game1.r for defining the bad event.
glossary.Game2: Intermediate game where the ciphertext is (f pk r, h) — the XOR masking with the message is removed, making the ciphertext independent of the challenge bit.
glossary.OWr: Local reformulation of the OW game where the challenge preimage x is a module variable rather than a local variable; syntactically equivalent to OW by sim.
glossary.Adv: CPA adversary type with two procedures: a1(pk) returns a pair of challenge messages, a2(c) returns a bit (guess of the challenge bit).
glossary.POracle: Partial oracle interface (queryable via o(x) but not initializable); the type of oracle access given to the adversary.
glossary.A_CPA: Adapter module converting a concrete CPA adversary (which receives bitstring ciphertexts) into an abstract CPA adversary (which receives rand * ptxt pairs) by applying the format operator (||).
glossary.CPA(O, S, A): Generic IND-CPA game parameterized by oracle O, scheme S, and adversary A; used in the concrete instantiation.
glossary.BR: Concrete Bellare-Rogaway scheme module implementing the Scheme interface with bitstring types and the (||) format operator.
glossary.Exp_OW(I): Concrete one-way experiment: samples keys and challenge, calls I.invert, checks if the returned value equals the preimage.
glossary.dptxt / drand / dkeys: Lossless uniform distributions over plaintexts, nonces, and keypairs respectively; their properties (lossless, full, uniform, funiform) are the key distribution assumptions.
glossary.f / fi: The trapdoor permutation family (f indexed by pkey) and its inverse (fi indexed by skey); the axiom fK states fi(sk, f(pk, x)) = x for valid keypairs.
glossary.(+^): Nilpotent (self-inverse) addition on plaintexts, modeling bitwise XOR: associative, commutative, and x +^ x +^ y = y.

_synthetic_module_bridges_note: br93 has no unrecorded module equivalences worth surfacing: every game-hop is codified as a local pr_* lemma (pr_Game0_Game1, pr_Game1_Game2, pr_bad_Game1_Game2, pr_Game2_OW), plus OW_OWr for the OW<->OWr reformulation. Unlike chacha_poly, there is no multi-wrapper reduction chain (ChaChaPoly -> OCC(I_stateless) -> OChaChaPoly(I)) that would justify a synthetic bridge. AUTO-BRIDGE-SUGGEST should fire on the rewrite_forms of the codified lemmas directly; no 2-step chain synthesis needed.
